+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Azra Gambit ## Chapter Three The transfer vessel's air filtration system hummed with a persistent mechanical drone. Arthur Neegan had spent eleven years listening to the wheeze of secondhand atmospheric processors in his habitat dome, and he could tell from the sound alone that this one was new, expensive, and entirely wasted on a passenger compartment containing exactly one passenger. He sat strapped into an acceleration couch upholstered in genuine leather—leather, forty million miles from the nearest cow—and considered the sequence of events that had deposited him here. They had been polite. That was the thing that unsettled him most. The six men who had descended on his plot in a gleaming silver flitter had worn sidearms with the casual indifference of men who never expected to draw them, and the officer—a trim, gray-templed man named Vorin—had actually apologized for the inconvenience. The insignia on their shoulders had been the crossed olive branches of the Antares Diplomatic Corps, which Arthur knew to be diplomatic in roughly the sense that a mining charge was a geological survey instrument. "You are not under arrest, Mr. Neegan," Vorin had said. "You are being extended an invitation." "And if I decline the invitation?" "Then we would be obliged to wait outside your dome until you reconsidered. We are very patient men, and we have brought sandwiches." So Arthur had gone. One did not argue with sandwiches and sidearms. Through the viewport, Mars had shrunk to a rust-colored coin, and ahead, growing steadily, hung a ship that was not on any registry Arthur knew—a long, dark spindle bristling with communication arrays and, he noted with professional interest, what were almost certainly weapon emplacements disguised as sensor pods. You could disguise the housing, but you couldn't disguise the heat radiators. Physics was a poor co-conspirator. The docking was flawless. The corridors beyond were carpeted. *Carpeted.* Arthur had not seen carpet since Earth. Vorin led him to a stateroom where a woman sat behind a desk of dark polished wood. She was perhaps sixty, with white hair cropped close, and eyes the color of slate. She rose and extended a hand. "Mr. Neegan. I am Ambassador Ila Renn. Sit down, please. Do you drink coffee? Real coffee, I mean, not the chicory paste they serve in Mariner City." "I'd be a fool to refuse." "You'd be a fool to refuse many things I'm going to offer you today. I'm pleased we agree on principle so early." She poured from a silver carafe. "Tell me, Mr. Neegan—what do you know about Azra?" "I'm a mining engineer. I know the extraction tolerances, the shielding requirements, the going rate per gram." "The going rate." She smiled thinly. "Yes. As of this morning, eleven thousand credits per gram. Do you know why?" "It accelerates radioactive decay. Catalytically. A few grams in a drive core and you get fission yields out of fuel that would otherwise sit inert for ten thousand years." "That is what Azra does, Mr. Neegan. It is not why it commands the price it does. Hydrogen fuses—that is what it does. Why it *matters* is that men built bombs with it." She set down his cup. "Any government that controls a sufficient Azra reserve controls the rate of interstellar expansion. They control which colonies get drives and which wither. They control which fleets move and which sit in dock burning dead uranium. Azra is not a metal. It is a *vote*—the only vote that counts—on the future arrangement of human space." "And I'm sitting on a large deposit of votes." "You are sitting, Mr. Neegan, on the largest contiguous vein yet surveyed. Your unassuming forty-hectare plot, leased for the price of a first-generation settler's passage debt, contains by our spectrographic estimates more Azra than the combined holdings of the three chartered corporations currently strip-mining the Tharsis fields with indentured labor." Arthur was quiet for a moment. The coffee was extraordinary. That, somehow, frightened him more than the weapon emplacements. "My mineral rights are registered," he said. "Filed with the Colonial Authority." "The Colonial Authority is a wholly owned instrument of whoever last paid its administrators. Your registration will survive precisely as long as no one important wants your land. I estimate—" she consulted nothing; she did not need to—"nine days. The corporations move slowly because they move legally. Others will not be so fastidious. There are already three survey flitters logging your plot from altitude. One of them is not corporate." "Whose is it?" "That, Mr. Neegan, is the question that brought a diplomatic vessel forty million miles. We believe it belongs to the Outer Combine. And the Outer Combine does not lease land. It removes obstacles." "Meaning me." "Meaning you." She folded her hands. "Now. My offer. Antares will recognize your claim—formally, with treaty weight behind it. You retain title. You retain ten percent of extraction yield in perpetuity, which will make you wealthier than the Colonial Governor by next spring. In exchange, Antares takes exclusive purchase rights, and you take our protection." "And the laborers? Your corporations work them on indenture, same as the rest." Something flickered in the slate eyes—surprise, perhaps, or recalculation. "You could write your own labor terms into the charter. Free contracts. It would cost you a fraction of a fortune you cannot currently spend anyway." "Why would you allow that?" "Because, Mr. Neegan, we need your signature more than we need your miners cheap. And because a man who asks that question first is a man whose signature stays bought." She slid a document folder across the wood. "I'll be candid. If you refuse, we return you home unharmed, and you will be dead or dispossessed within a month. I take no pleasure in that arithmetic. But arithmetic does not require my pleasure." Arthur looked at the folder. He looked at the viewport, where Mars hung small and red and impossibly far from anyone who cared what happened to one engineer. "I'd want it in writing," he said slowly, "that the labor clause can't be amended without my consent." The Ambassador smiled, and for the first time it reached her eyes. "Mr. Neegan," she said, "I believe we are going to get along."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Asimovian style and prompt requirements. The author successfully captures the classic sci-fi tone with clean, precise prose and the characteristic blend of intimate human drama against vast political machinations. The worldbuilding is particularly effective, establishing the stakes and scope through natural dialogue and observation rather than exposition dumps. The characterization is nuanced, especially Ambassador Renn, who emerges as a sophisticated political operator - neither villain nor savior, but a pragmatic player in a larger game. Arthur's responses feel authentic to his background as a mining engineer suddenly thrust into galactic politics. His concern for laborers adds moral complexity without feeling forced. The dialogue carries the narrative effectively, revealing character motivations and political tensions organically. Renn's speech patterns convey authority and calculation, while Arthur's responses show a man trying to maintain agency in an impossible situation. The exchange about labor conditions particularly demonstrates both characters' values and tactical thinking. The prose maintains elegance without becoming overwrought. Details like "leather, forty million miles from the nearest cow" and the observation about heat radiators reveal character perspective while building the world. The pacing builds tension appropriately, moving from Arthur's disorientation to the high-stakes negotiation. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the polite-but-threatening escort, the luxurious ship contrasted with colonial austerity, and the powerful figure making an offer that can't be refused are familiar tropes. The ending, while not inappropriately positive, resolves perhaps too neatly given the established dangers. The piece succeeds in establishing the larger intrigue while maintaining focus on Arthur's immediate dilemma. The political complexity feels authentic to the Asimovian tradition of examining how individuals navigate vast institutional forces. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 87.2
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Darkroom Revelations The red light makes everything look like the inside of a heartbeat. I've thought this every session for the past three weeks, and every session I almost say it out loud, and every session I don't, because Elliot is standing close enough that I can hear him breathe, and some things feel too obvious to speak in a room this small. He's rocking the print in the developer bath with those long fingers, black nail polish chipped at the edges, and I'm watching him instead of the image blooming up through the chemicals. This is becoming a problem. I'm supposed to be the one who knows what she's doing—technically and otherwise. I'm twenty-five. I've dated firmly. Butch women who arm-wrestled me for the check. A boyfriend in undergrad who looked like he was carved out of certainty. I like definition. I like knowing what I'm looking at. Elliot is not definition. Elliot is a question mark in a thrifted mesh shirt and a silver chain he told me belonged to his grandmother, hair falling in his eyes, a boy built entirely out of soft edges and sudden, devastating observations about Francesca Woodman. "Dani," he says, and his voice does the thing it does—quiet, like he's asking permission to exist in the sentence. "Can I tell you something weird?" "Weird is my entire aesthetic." I keep my voice easy. I've learned that if I come on too strong, he folds inward like a fern. So I've been practicing this—this restraint, which is not a muscle I've ever used. It aches in an interesting way. He lifts the print with the tongs, slides it into the stop bath. The image is one of his self-portraits—double exposure, his face overlaid with lace curtains from his apartment. He watches it instead of me. "I've been thinking I might not be—" He stops. The timer ticks. "A guy. Exactly. Or not only. I don't know. I keep trying to find the edge of it and there isn't one." The red light hums. My heart does something complicated and embarrassing. "Okay," I say carefully. "Thank you for telling me." "That's it? Okay?" He laughs, shaky. "I've been rehearsing that for two weeks." "Did you want me to gasp?" I bump his shoulder with mine, gentle, gentle. "Elliot. Look at your work." I gesture at the prints drying on the line above us—his body dissolving into curtains, into flowers, into light. "You've been telling us all semester. You just hadn't said it in words yet." He goes still. In the red glow I can see his eyes get bright and wet, and every instinct I have screams to take over—pull him in, say *I've got you*, fix it, hold it, manage it. That's what I do. I take charge. People like that about me. I like that about me. But this isn't mine to take charge of. This is his country, and I'm just lucky to be standing at the border. "It looks good on you," I say instead. "The not-knowing." He wipes his eyes with the back of his wrist, smearing nothing because he isn't wearing eyeliner tonight, which I only notice now, and notice that I noticed. "Does it freak you out?" "Why would it freak me out?" "Because—" He hesitates, and the air in the room changes pressure, and oh, we're doing this, we're actually doing this. "Because I think you look at me. Sometimes. The way I look at you." I have never in my life not had an answer ready. I open my mouth and what comes out is the truth, which is unprofessional of my mouth: "It freaks me out a little. Not you. Me. I always thought I knew exactly what I was into. Sharp lines. People who are *definitely* something." I laugh, and it sounds younger than I am. "And then you walked into crit in that ridiculous velvet coat and I haven't known anything since." "The coat isn't ridiculous." "The coat is incredible, that's the problem." He smiles—the real one, the one that takes over his whole face—and steps toward me, and I step toward him, and his elbow catches the box on the counter. The box of photo paper. The *open* box of photo paper, because one of us, probably me, probably an hour ago when I was distracted by his hands, didn't close it properly. It tips, sheets fanning across the counter under the safelight—fine—except the door to the loading room is cracked, and a blade of white hallway light is lying across the counter like a crime scene. "Oh no," Elliot breathes. "Oh no, that's—" "Forty dollars of paper. Yep." I scoop the sheets together, but we both know. Fogged. Ghosted. Useless. "Dead on arrival." He's apologizing, flustered, and I watch him spiral for exactly three seconds before I do take charge, because some things are mine to take charge of. I put my hands on his shoulders. "Hey. Elliot. It's paper. We exposed it to light and now it's changed and it can't go back." I hear what I'm saying as I say it. So does he. "...Okay, the metaphor is a little much." "It's so much," he agrees, laughing, and his hands come up to rest against my forearms, tentative, asking. "We could print on it anyway," he says. "See what comes through the fog." "Experimental photography," I say. "Technically it's the assignment." We stand there in the heartbeat-red light, ruined paper between us, both of us exposed, both of us changed, neither of us going back. And when he leans in, I let him be the one to close the distance—the hardest, easiest thing I've ever done. The kiss is soft and uncertain and completely undefined. Turns out I'm into that too.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive yet introspective character navigating unfamiliar emotional territory. The first-person perspective feels authentic, with internal monologue that reveals character depth without becoming overly expository. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically. The red light creates an intimate, confessional atmosphere that serves the story's emotional needs while the photographic processes provide organic metaphors for transformation and revelation. The author shows restraint in not over-explaining these parallels, allowing them to emerge naturally. Character development is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her usual take-charge nature and her desire to give Elliot agency feels genuine and well-motivated. Her attraction is portrayed with complexity—she's drawn to Elliot precisely because he challenges her usual preferences for "definition." Elliot's vulnerability and tentative self-discovery are handled with sensitivity, avoiding stereotypes while maintaining authenticity. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, capturing the awkwardness of intimate revelations without becoming stilted. Conversations flow organically, with realistic interruptions and hesitations. The pacing builds tension effectively toward the climactic moment of connection. The prose style is confident without being showy. Sentences vary in length and structure, creating good rhythm. Imagery is vivid but not overwrought—descriptions like "the red light makes everything look like the inside of a heartbeat" are evocative without being purple. The ending feels earned rather than forced. The accidental exposure of the photo paper provides a perfect metaphor for the characters' own exposure and transformation, but it doesn't feel contrived because it grows naturally from the darkroom setting and their distracted state. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphors become slightly heavy-handed (though the characters themselves acknowledge this), and some descriptions that border on precious. The piece also relies somewhat on familiar tropes of the sensitive, questioning youth and the older, more experienced partner, though it handles these with enough nuance to avoid cliché. + Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Stolen Moments in Roma **8:47 AM — Trevi Fountain** The Vespa missed him by a breath. Ben felt the wind of it, dropped his guidebook into a puddle, and stumbled backward into a souvenir cart, sending plastic Colosseums clattering across the cobblestones. "*Ma sei scemo?* You walk with your eyes in a book in *Roma*?" The rider yanked off her helmet, releasing a tumble of dark hair and a torrent of Italian that Ben understood perhaps four percent of. "I'm—terribly sorry—*mi dispiace*—*molto*—" He bent to gather the souvenirs, knocking his head against the cart. "*Scusi*. To you. And to the cart." She stopped mid-tirade. Stared. Then laughed—a sharp, delighted bark. "You apologize to the *cart*?" "It seemed... involved." She circled her motorcycle—a battered Ducati with a long scratch down the fuel tank, painted over badly in turquoise nail polish, like a scar someone had decided to celebrate rather than hide. She picked up his sodden guidebook between two fingers. "*Rome in Three Days.* This is your plan? Three days?" "Three months, actually. Sabbatical. I'm working through it methodically—" "Methodically." She said the word like it tasted of medicine. Then she tossed the book into the fountain. It hit the water with a flat slap, beside the coins and the wishes. "That was—" Ben blinked. "That was a library book." "Now it belongs to Rome." She held out a helmet that had appeared, somehow, from nowhere. "Get on. I will show you the real city. I am Alessandra." "Ben. I really shouldn't—" "Correct. You should not. Get on." --- **10:15 AM — Campo de' Fiori** She moved through the market like weather. Ben trailed behind, watching her argue with a fruit vendor about figs in dialect so rapid it sounded like applause. When they walked away, she handed him a peach he was fairly certain hadn't been paid for. "Did you—" "Eat your peach, *professore*." He ate the peach. It was, he had to admit, the best peach of his life, sweetened slightly by guilt. "What do you teach?" she asked. "Medieval manuscripts. Marginalia, specifically. The doodles monks left in the margins." "So you study people who were bored at work." She nodded approvingly. "Finally, an honest academic." --- **12:30 PM — A rooftop he was fairly sure they weren't allowed on** They'd climbed through a service door Alessandra had opened with a hairpin and total confidence. Rome spread below them, terracotta and gold, the dome of St. Peter's hazy in the heat. "My grandmother could see this roof from her window," Alessandra said, suddenly quieter. "She told me Rome doesn't belong to the people who own it. It belongs to the people who climb it." "That's rather beautiful." "She also stole a German officer's motorcycle in 1944, so." A shrug. "We are a family of strong opinions about property." Ben looked at her sideways. "The scratch on your Ducati. The turquoise one." "Mm?" "What's the story?" She smiled at the skyline and said nothing at all, which was somehow worse than any answer. --- **3:00 PM — Trastevere** He noticed it in the little antique shop: the way her hand drifted over a brass compass, and then the compass simply wasn't there anymore. Sleight of hand so fluid it looked like affection. Outside, she pressed it into his palm. "Alessandra. You can't—I can't—" "It's a compass, Ben. For a man who has lost his guidebook." Her eyes were bright, daring him. "You can return it. Go. Apologize to the shop like you apologized to the cart." He stood there, the brass warming in his hand, every cell of his upbringing screaming. Then he put it in his pocket. Her grin was sunrise. "*Bravo.* There is a criminal in there after all. Very small. Like a mouse. But there." "I'm returning it tomorrow," he said weakly. "Of course you are." --- **6:00 PM — The Ducati, somewhere along the Tiber** She drove too fast, weaving through traffic with her knees, and Ben—arms around her waist, heart in his throat—realized he had stopped being terrified somewhere around the fourth near-death experience and had started, alarmingly, to laugh. "You're laughing!" she shouted over her shoulder. "Madonna, we ruined you in one day!" "I'm reassessing my entire methodology!" "That is the most romantic thing an Englishman has ever said!" --- **9:40 PM — Ponte Sisto** The river was black silk under the lamps. They leaned on the bridge railing, shoulders almost touching, eating gelato she had—miraculously—actually paid for. "Why did you take me with you today?" Ben asked. Alessandra considered the water for a long moment. "You apologized to a cart," she said finally. "I thought: this man has never stolen one single thing in his life. Not an apple. Not an hour. Not even a moment." She turned to him, and for once the mischief in her face had gone still, replaced by something he couldn't name. "It seemed like an emergency." "And now?" "Now you have a stolen compass and a stolen day." She stepped closer. "What will you do with them?" He kissed her. Or she kissed him—afterward he could never reconstruct the order of events, only that it happened, and that it tasted of pistachio and recklessness, and that the Tiber kept flowing underneath them as if nothing had changed when in fact everything had. When they broke apart, she was already pulling on her helmet, already retreating into motion, into mystery. "Tomorrow," she said, swinging onto the Ducati. "Piazza Navona. Nine o'clock. Don't bring a book." "How do I know you'll come?" She kicked the engine to life and grinned at him over the turquoise scar on the tank. "You don't," she called, and roared off into the Roman night. Ben stood on the bridge, disheveled, disoriented, and grinning like a fool. He reached into his pocket for the compass. It was gone. In its place: a single turquoise hairpin, and a scrap of paper that read, in looping hand— *Now you have to come find me. —A.*
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in adapting the prompt's requirements. The author successfully creates a day-long Roman adventure structured as interconnected vignettes, each with distinct timestamps that provide clear progression while maintaining narrative momentum. The characterization is particularly well-handled. Alessandra emerges as a vivid, complex figure whose kleptomania is woven naturally into the narrative rather than stated outright - from the unpaid peach to the stolen compass to her grandmother's wartime motorcycle theft, creating a believable family legacy of "strong opinions about property." Ben's transformation from bumbling academic to willing accomplice feels organic, driven by genuine attraction to Alessandra's freedom rather than sudden personality change. The dialogue crackles with authenticity and cultural specificity. Alessandra's voice captures Italian directness and passion ("You apologize to the cart?"), while Ben's overly polite British nature comes through in his compulsive apologizing, even to inanimate objects. The language mixing feels natural rather than forced, and the conversations reveal character efficiently. The prose style is confident and evocative without becoming overwrought. Descriptions like "Rome spread below them, terracotta and gold" and "the river was black silk under the lamps" create atmosphere without purple excess. The author shows particular skill in physical details that reveal character - the turquoise nail polish covering the Ducati's scratch becomes a recurring motif that pays off cleverly in the ending. The pacing builds effectively through increasingly daring scenarios, from the market theft to the rooftop trespass to Ben's willing participation in keeping the compass. The emotional arc feels earned - Ben's gradual loosening up is motivated by genuine attraction and the intoxicating freedom Alessandra represents. The ending strikes an excellent balance between resolution and open possibility. The compass switcheroo is both surprising and inevitable given Alessandra's established character, while the note creates anticipation for future encounters without forcing false closure. Minor weaknesses include some slightly predictable romantic beats and moments where the cultural contrasts border on stereotypical, though these are handled with enough nuance to avoid serious problems. The piece maintains strong momentum throughout without significant meandering. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter Three: Vega, Neat I'm polishing a glass that doesn't need polishing when she walks in. You learn to read entrances in this job. Miners shuffle. Merchants stride. Smugglers slide in sideways like they're hoping the doorframe will forget them. This one—tentacled dreads frizzed out like she's been running her hands through them for three days straight, jacket buttoned wrong—she doesn't walk in so much as wash ashore. She plops onto the stool at the far end of the bar, away from the dart game and Old Marsten's nightly argument with the jukebox. "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." "Rough cycle?" I pour. The whisky catches the low light, amber and honest. "Rough decade." She wraps both hands around the glass like it might float away. One of her dreads twitches—Ophiuchan, then, and stressed enough that her tells are showing. "You want food with that? Kitchen does a decent algae melt. 'Decent' is doing some heavy lifting in that sentence, but it's hot." That gets a flicker of a smile. "Just the whisky. And maybe... quiet?" "Quiet I can do." I drift down the bar, top off Marsten's ale, pretend to inventory the bitters. The thing about quiet, though, is that nobody who asks for it actually wants it. They want a witness. So when she clears her throat four minutes later, I'm not surprised. "This place. The Last Outpost. Cute name." "Owner thought so. It's technically the second-to-last outpost, but 'The Penultimate Outpost' didn't fit on the sign." She laughs—a startled, rusty sound, like she forgot she could. "You get a lot of traffic through here?" "Miners, mostly. Ore haulers. The occasional pilgrim who took a wrong turn at the Veil Nebula." I lean against the back counter, casual as anything, watching her dreads twitch in sequence now. "Why? Looking for someone?" Her glass stops halfway to her mouth. "Avoiding someone," she says quietly. "Ah." I nod like I haven't lived that exact sentence. "Well. We're good at avoiding here. It's our primary export, after iron and bad decisions." "You don't understand." She leans forward, voice dropping. "I worked for the Calloway Combine. Logistics. Boring stuff—manifests, cargo weights, routing. Except three weeks ago I noticed the weights didn't match. Containers listed as ore that massed like... like something else." My polishing rag goes still. The Combine runs half the shipping lanes in this sector. They also, rumor holds, run things that don't appear on any manifest. "You should maybe not be telling the bartender this," I say. "Bartenders are like priests. Confidential." "Bartenders are like bartenders. We gossip professionally." "Then I'm trusting you to be bad at your job." She knocks back half the double. "I copied the manifests. I don't even know why. Insurance, maybe. Stupidity, probably. And then I ran. And now there's a Combine enforcement vessel two systems back, and it's been two systems back for six days, and I am running out of systems." Somewhere behind me, a glass shatters—Dex at table four, knocking his tumbler off with one of his four elbows again. The stranger nearly comes out of her skin, half off the stool, hand diving into her jacket. "Easy," I say, raising both palms. "That's just Dex. He's got too many elbows and not enough coordination. Happens twice a week, minimum." "Sorry, Nets!" Dex calls cheerfully, already collecting shards. She settles back, breathing hard. Her hand comes out of the jacket empty, but I clocked the shape of what's in there. So she's armed. Wonderful. "Nets?" she asks, voice still shaky. "Nettle. Nets to friends. You're trending toward friend, mostly because you've made my evening interesting, which I will probably regret." "Sayuri." She extends a hand. Her grip is clammy. "And I'll be gone by morning. I just need somewhere to sit until the fuel depot opens. Then I'm dust." I should say *fine, sit, drink, go.* That's the smart play. I came to this rock specifically to stop collecting other people's trouble, having generated a lifetime supply of my own. Instead I hear myself say: "The depot's closed till oh-six-hundred. And Vance, who runs it, reports every fueling to the Combine commerce office. Standard kickback arrangement." Sayuri goes pale in a way that does interesting things to Ophiuchan skin tone, sort of a gray-green tide. "Then I'm dead." "You're not dead. You're complicated. There's a difference, though admittedly the difference is mostly me." I pour her another finger of Vega, on the house. "There's an old ore tug in dock seven. Belongs to nobody since Hettie passed. It still flies, and nobody reports anything about dock seven because officially dock seven doesn't exist." She stares at me. "Why would you help me? You don't know me. I could be lying about all of it." "Could be." I shrug. "But your dreads twitch when you're scared, not when you talk about the manifests. Whatever's in your jacket pocket, you've never drawn it in anger—you grabbed for it backwards. And you button your jacket wrong, which means you dressed running, in the dark, probably while crying." I slide the glass to her. "Liars are tidier." "You read people like that often?" "Used to do it for a living. Now I do it for tips." Sayuri's eyes narrow, and for the first time tonight she looks at *me* properly. "What did you do? Before this?" "That," I say, picking my rag back up, "is a story that costs more than a double Vega." The door chimes. Two figures step in—matte gray uniforms, Combine sigils on the shoulders, eyes sweeping the room with the methodical boredom of people paid to find things. Sayuri freezes solid. "Drink your whisky," I murmur, smiling my best dim-bartender smile at the newcomers. "Slowly. And whatever you do, don't touch your jacket." "Nets—" "Evening, officers!" I call out, bright as a supernova. "First round's on the house."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the cozy sci-fi atmosphere requested. The author successfully establishes the setting of The Last Outpost as a lived-in space station bar, populated with regulars like Old Marsten and Dex, creating an authentic sense of community that serves the "cozy" requirement well. The characterization of Nets is particularly well-executed. Her voice comes through clearly in the first-person narration - world-weary but not cynical, observant, and carrying hints of a mysterious past without being heavy-handed about it. Her dialogue feels natural and reveals character through subtext, particularly in lines like "Bartenders are like bartenders. We gossip professionally" and her assessment of Sayuri's tells. The dialogue achieves the requested 60% ratio effectively and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building tension. The exchanges feel authentic to the setting and characters, avoiding exposition dumps while still conveying necessary information about the Combine threat and Sayuri's predicament. The conflict setup is well-structured, introducing Sayuri's flight from the Combine and escalating with the arrival of enforcement officers at the chapter's end. This creates genuine tension without resolving anything, as requested. The pacing builds naturally from quiet character establishment to mounting danger. The world-building is economical but effective, dropping in details like "Ophiuchan" physiology and the Veil Nebula without over-explaining. The author shows restraint in not drowning the narrative in sci-fi terminology while still establishing the setting as distinctly futuristic. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious, particularly in some of the more elaborate metaphors. The piece also relies somewhat on familiar tropes (mysterious bartender with a past, fugitive seeking help), though it executes them competently. The emotional complexity is present but subtle - Nets' internal conflict between self-preservation and helping others, Sayuri's desperation masked by attempted composure. The writing maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing tension with the cozy atmosphere through humor and character interactions. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 15.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 82.5
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gone South The saxophone finds me before I find anything else. It's coming from somewhere down Decatur Street, this low, mournful sound that wraps around the morning humidity like it owns it. I've been awake for twenty-six hours. My phone is at 12%. I have $43 and a backpack containing two shirts, a phone charger, and a granola bar I'm rationing like it's the apocalypse. Which, in a way, it is. *You did this,* the voice in my head says. It sounds like Mom. *You blew up your whole life over—* Over being honest. Over saying four words at the dinner table. The casserole had gotten cold while Dad stared at me like I'd announced I was joining a cult, and Mom had cried the kind of crying that's somehow about *her*. So. New Orleans. Because the trucker was going to New Orleans, and because it was the opposite direction of everything. The street smells like powdered sugar and old beer and something green and rotting underneath. People flow around me—tourists in matching shirts, a man walking a parrot, a woman in a sequined dress at nine in the morning like that's normal. Maybe here it is. Nobody looks at me twice, and I can't tell if that feels like freedom or like drowning. I'm counting my money for the third time when a voice says, "You're doing that wrong." I look up. A kid my age—maybe seventeen—is sitting on a stoop with a skateboard across their knees and a dog that looks like it was assembled from spare parts. Buzzed hair, a denim jacket covered in safety pins, eyes that have already done the math on me. "Doing what wrong?" "Counting your money in public. In the Quarter. Might as well wear a sign." They pat the dog. "First day?" "That obvious?" "You've got the look. Like a deer that walked into a casino." They stand, sticking out a hand. "Crescent. Like the city. Not my real name, before you ask." "Taylor." I pause. "Actually my real name. As of recently." Something flickers in Crescent's face—recognition, maybe. They don't ask. That alone makes my throat go tight, because back home everybody *asks*, everybody needs you to explain yourself like a math problem. "C'mon," they say. "I'll show you where the church does lunch. You eat free, nobody calls anybody." --- I follow them. I know I shouldn't follow strangers. I know how this story goes in every after-school special. But the saxophone is still playing somewhere behind us, and the granola bar is half gone, and Crescent walks like they have somewhere to be, which is more than I've got. "Rule one," they say over their shoulder. "Don't sleep in the Quarter. Cops sweep it. Rule two, if anybody offers you a place to crash and they're over twenty-five, the answer is no. Doesn't matter how nice they seem. *Especially* if they seem nice." "Why are you helping me?" Crescent shrugs. "Somebody did it for me. Pay it backward, or whatever." "How long have you been out here?" "Eight months." They say it flat, the way you'd report the weather. "Your family—" "Don't." Not angry. Just a door closing. "We don't do origin stories out here. Everybody's running from something. You don't gotta know what." But I catch it later, at the church—a long table, red beans and rice, a volunteer who calls everyone "baby." Crescent's sleeve rides up when they reach for the bread, and there's a hospital bracelet, plastic, worn soft. They see me see it and yank the sleeve down, and for one second their whole face is somebody else's face. Younger. Scared. "Don't," they say again. Quieter this time. "I wasn't going to." "Yeah you were. Everybody does." They stab at the rice. "I'm not crazy. I'm just— it's complicated." "Two days ago my dad told me I was confused and my mom cried about what the *neighbors* would think," I say. "I know complicated." Crescent looks at me for a long moment. Then, the ghost of a smile: "Tennessee?" "How'd you—" "The accent, babe. You sound like sweet tea." --- The afternoon stretches strange and golden. Crescent shows me where to fill a water bottle, which library lets you charge your phone, which corner store guy will pretend not to see you take a banana. I am learning a whole curriculum that no school counselor with her smug folder of "concerning behavioral patterns" could ever grade. My phone buzzes. Mom. Fourteen missed calls now. One text: *Please just tell me you're alive.* I stare at it until the screen goes dark. "You gonna answer?" Crescent asks. We're sitting by the river now, the Mississippi sliding by all brown and enormous, a steamboat bellowing somewhere. "I don't know what to say." "Say you're alive." They pull their knees up. "I didn't, with mine. Now I don't know if I can." Their voice does something complicated. "Eight months, the door just gets heavier, you know? Every day you don't call adds a pound." "Is that your secret? The thing we don't do origin stories about?" "One of 'em." They flick a pebble into the water. "Look. The street'll keep you. But it keeps you like a pawn shop keeps your stuff. Eventually it sells pieces of you off." They look at me sideways. "You're two days in. Your door's still light. That's all I'm saying." The sun is going down over the river, turning everything copper. Somewhere behind us, impossibly, the saxophone again—or another one, this city must grow them like weeds—playing something that sounds like grief and gladness having an argument. I type: *I'm alive. I'm safe. I'm still Taylor. That part doesn't change.* My thumb hovers. "Hey," Crescent says. "Whatever you decide. You're not invented drama, okay? You're the real deal." I don't know how they knew to say exactly that. I press send.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in depicting a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The writing successfully captures Taylor's voice through a mix of vulnerability, defiance, and observational wit that feels genuinely adolescent without being patronizing. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor emerges as a complex protagonist - neither purely victim nor rebel, but someone making desperate choices with realistic consequences. Crescent serves as an effective foil and guide, with their own implied trauma creating natural tension and depth. The dialogue feels authentic to the characters' ages and circumstances, avoiding both overly mature philosophical discourse and stereotypical teen speak. The New Orleans setting is rendered with sensory specificity - the saxophone, powdered sugar and beer smells, the humidity - without falling into tourist-brochure clichés. The author demonstrates restraint in world-building, focusing on details relevant to a runaway's experience rather than general atmosphere. The emotional complexity is well-handled. Taylor's conflicted feelings about family, identity, and survival create genuine internal tension. The piece avoids both excessive melodrama and unrealistic stoicism. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - offering a moment of connection and small hope without resolving Taylor's larger predicament. However, some elements feel slightly convenient. Crescent appears at precisely the right moment with exactly the right knowledge and attitude. The church lunch and helpful resources are perhaps too readily available. The pacing occasionally rushes through what could be more developed scenes, particularly the initial meeting between the characters. The prose style is generally strong, with effective use of short, punchy sentences mixed with longer, more reflective passages. Some metaphors work well ("like a deer that walked into a casino"), while others feel slightly forced. The mixed tense approach works naturally, reflecting the character's mental state. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 79.8
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# My Apocalypse Buddy The end of the world, Midas had discovered, was mostly a logistics problem. It wasn't the radiation, or the roving gangs, or the weather that had developed Opinions. It was that nobody had thought to leave behind a manual, and the only surviving repository of pre-Collapse wisdom within forty miles was a stuffed koala with a personality chip designed for children aged three to seven. "Today's forecast," announced Chewy from his perch on the rusted filing cabinet, "is *sunny with a chance of FUN!*" "It's raining," said Midas. "My weather module is aspirational." Midas peered out through the gap in the corrugated wall of what had once been, according to the sign, BARRY'S DISCOUNT TYRES & EXHAUST. Barry had not survived. Neither had most of the tyres, which said something about the priorities of civilization's collapse. "We need food," Midas said. "We have half a tin of something labeled 'meat-adjacent' and a packet of crackers I'm fairly sure achieved sentience last Tuesday." "I voted to keep the crackers as pets." "You don't get a vote. You run on a potato battery." "A potato battery *and* dreams, Midas. A potato battery and dreams." The plan—and Midas used the word in its loosest, most apologetic sense—was to scavenge the old retail park to the east. It had been picked over a dozen times, but desperation has a way of making you check the same cupboards repeatedly, like a man who has lost his keys and his civilization in the same afternoon. He packed the essentials: rope, knife, the lucky bottle cap, and Chewy, who rode in the top of the rucksack like a small, smug figurehead. --- The retail park was a monument to optimism. Someone, in the before-times, had genuinely believed humanity needed a shop that sold only candles. The candle shop had, ironically, burned down. It was on the wall of the former Sofa Warehouse that Midas saw it: fresh paint, which in itself was remarkable. Paint required someone alive to apply it, and someone alive with spare time was practically aristocracy. The graffiti read: **THE GREEN PLACE LIVES. FOLLOW THE CROWS TO THE WATER THAT REMEMBERS. SANCTUARY FOR ALL WHO ASK NICELY.** Below it, a crude arrow pointed north, toward the wilds. "Sanctuary," Midas breathed. "Statistically," said Chewy, "ninety percent of cryptic graffiti is a trap, and the other ten percent is teenagers." "It says 'ask nicely.' Murderers don't care about manners." "The *politest* people I've ever met were murderers. There was a lovely man in Sector Twelve who apologized to everyone he—" "We're going." "I want it noted in the official record that I objected." "There is no official record." "Then I shall maintain one. *Day one: Midas is an idiot.*" --- Following the crows turned out to be harder than advertised, principally because crows, lacking any sense of narrative obligation, flew in all directions at once. Midas picked the largest and most purposeful-looking one and followed it for three miles before it landed on a dead sheep and began, with great purpose, to eat it. "The water that remembers," Midas muttered, consulting the horizon. "What does water remember?" "Everything you did to it," said Chewy darkly. "Water never forgets." What the water remembered, it transpired, was being a reservoir. They found it at the bottom of a valley, gray and vast and patient, and on its shore stood a cluster of shelters with—Midas's heart leapt—*smoke* rising from them. Actual cooking smoke. The smell of stew drifted up the hillside like a promise. He was halfway down the slope, rehearsing his nicest asking voice, when the ground gave way. It was, he reflected mid-tumble, a very *deliberate* sort of giving way—the kind involving a tarpaulin, a pit, and someone's weekend project. He landed in mud. Chewy landed on his face. "Day one, addendum," said the koala. "*Told him so.*" Faces appeared at the pit's rim. They belonged to a wary, weathered family—a woman with a crossbow and the expression of someone who had been disappointed by strangers professionally. "You read the sign?" she demanded. "Yes! Sanctuary! I'm asking nicely. Please. Very nicely. Tremendously nicely." "That sign's bait. We catch raiders with it. Decent folk are suspicious of free sanctuary. Only greedy idiots follow it." "He's not greedy," offered Chewy helpfully. "Just an idiot." There was a silence of the kind that occurs when armed strangers realize a stuffed koala has spoken. "That toy talked." "He does that," sighed Midas. "He also keeps records, apparently." The woman lowered the crossbow a fraction. In a world without television, a talking koala with a sarcasm protocol was entertainment of the highest order. "It tell jokes?" "Madam," said Chewy, with dignity, "I contain four hundred jokes suitable for children aged three to seven, and after fifty years of degraded memory storage, most of them are now *accidentally horrifying*." --- They were released at dusk, in exchange for two hours of Chewy's repertoire ("Why did the chicken cross the road? *The road is gone. Everything is gone.* Knock knock."), Midas's rope, his knife, his lucky bottle cap, and a solemn promise never to return without bringing crackers. The walk home took six hours, two of which were spent hiding from something in the dark that breathed like a broken accordion. Midas twisted his ankle in a rabbit hole. It began raining with renewed creative energy. Somewhere around midnight, his left boot achieved independence and was lost to the mud. BARRY'S DISCOUNT TYRES & EXHAUST had never looked so beautiful. Midas collapsed onto his nest of insulation foam, one-booted, ropeless, knifeless, luckless, and ate the meat-adjacent substance cold, sharing none of it because his only companion ran on a potato. "Final entry, day one," said Chewy softly, from the filing cabinet. "Mission failed. Supplies lost. Dignity unrecoverable." A pause. The little servos whirred. "But we came home together. Forecast for tomorrow: sunny. With a chance of fun." "It's still raining, Chewy." "Aspirational, Midas. Aspirational."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, observational humor, and philosophical undertones wrapped in absurdist situations. The author successfully creates a post-apocalyptic world that feels both bleak and oddly cozy, much like Pratchett's Discworld manages to be both satirical and warm. The characterization is particularly effective. Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose optimism consistently leads him into trouble, while Chewy provides an excellent foil with his sardonic commentary and malfunctioning cheerfulness. The dynamic between them feels authentic and drives much of the story's humor. Chewy's personality—a children's toy with degraded programming spouting accidentally horrifying jokes—is genuinely creative and well-executed. The prose style successfully mimics Pratchett's voice with its parenthetical observations, dry asides, and philosophical musings disguised as throwaway lines. Phrases like "the end of the world was mostly a logistics problem" and "weather that had developed Opinions" capture that distinctive Pratchett flavor of finding the mundane absurdity in extraordinary circumstances. The story structure follows the prompt requirements well, establishing the mission, showing how it goes wrong, and returning the characters home worse for wear. The plot progression feels natural and the complications arise organically from the characters' actions and the world's logic. However, there are some weaknesses. The middle section with the pit trap and the family feels slightly rushed, and their motivations could be clearer. The ending, while thematically appropriate, borders on being too neat in its hopefulness given the harsh realities established earlier. Some of the humor occasionally feels forced rather than emerging naturally from the situation. The dialogue is generally strong, particularly Chewy's lines, though Midas sometimes serves more as a straight man than a fully realized character. The descriptive passages effectively build the world without becoming overwrought, maintaining the story's pace and tone throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 8 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Shelter from the Storm The bell above the door of Pendragon Books jangled violently as a man in dark sunglasses and an expensive coat stumbled inside, pressing his back against the shelves like a fugitive in a war film. "Could you—" he gasped, "could you possibly lock that?" Eleri Morgan looked up from her ledger, pencil still poised. "It's half eleven on a Tuesday. We're open." "Yes, but there's a small army of photographers about to descend on your charming high street, and I'd rather they didn't follow me in." "Photographers. In Llanfair." She set down her pencil. "Have the sheep done something newsworthy?" He pulled off the sunglasses with a flourish that was clearly practiced, revealing a face she recognized from approximately four hundred bus shelter posters. Rhys Trevelyan. *That* Rhys Trevelyan. He waited, with the expression of a man who has just produced a dove from a hat. "Oh," said Eleri. "You're an actor or something, aren't you?" A flicker—just a flicker—of something wounded crossed his face. "Or something. Yes. *Dragonfire*? *The Last Cartographer*? I was nominated for a BAFTA." "Nominated," she repeated, returning to her ledger. "Door's got a latch if you want it. Don't break it." He turned the latch, then drifted toward her counter with the loose-limbed confidence of a man accustomed to rooms rearranging themselves around him. "You genuinely don't know who I am." "I genuinely have stock to count." "Most people scream a bit. Or cry. One woman in Cardiff fainted, though to be fair it was very hot in that Tesco." "I'll try to faint later if there's time. We close at five." He laughed—a real laugh, surprised out of him—and leaned against the counter. "What's your name, then, since you've got mine?" "You haven't actually told me your name. You just listed your CV." "Rhys. Trevelyan." "Eleri Morgan. This is my shop. You're leaning on my new arrivals." He straightened, examined the stack, and held up a slim volume of R.S. Thomas. "Bleak choice for the front counter." "He's Welsh, he's brilliant, and tourists buy him to look serious on the train home. Don't crease the spine." "You know, I'm filming up at the castle for three weeks. Period piece. I play a doomed prince. Very brooding, lots of standing on ramparts." He set the book down carefully, watching her. "You could come watch. I could get you onto set." "Why would I want to watch you stand on ramparts?" "People generally do." "People generally have less inventory." He circled the shop slowly, trailing fingers along the shelves, and she watched him in the convex mirror she kept for shoplifters—watched him longer than strictly necessary, noting the way he moved like the room belonged to him, hating slightly that it worked on her. When he glanced up, she snapped her eyes back to the ledger. "You're pretending to count," he said. "I'm counting." "Your lips aren't moving and you haven't turned a page in two minutes." "Some of us can count silently. It's called school." He came back to the counter and, unexpectedly, his shoulders dropped. The performance dimmed like a stage light on a fader. "Can I tell you something strange? You're the first person in about six years who hasn't wanted anything from me. It's slightly terrifying." "I want you to stop leaning on my new arrivals again." "Besides that." He turned the Thomas volume over in his hands. "When I was nineteen, I auditioned for drama school in London. Welsh accent thick as bara brith. The panel laughed. Not cruelly—worse than cruelly. *Indulgently.* One of them said, 'Lovely, but we can't hear past the valleys.' So I went home and I murdered it. My own voice. Spent two years with recordings, training it out of me syllable by syllable. My mam said I came back sounding like the shipping forecast." He paused. "She died three years ago, and at the funeral I gave the eulogy in a voice she never knew. I keep thinking—if everyone stops clapping, what's actually left? I sold the original off for parts." The shop was very quiet. Outside, somewhere distant, a gull complained. "That's the first true thing you've said since you came in," Eleri said softly. "Probably, yes." "It was better than the BAFTA bit." "*Nominated*," he said, and they both smiled at the same time, and she felt the smile go somewhere dangerous in her chest and busied herself straightening the pencil pot. "The accent comes back, you know," she said. "Three weeks in Llanfair, you'll be singing in chapel by the end of it. We're contagious." "Is that a promise?" "It's a warning." She pulled the Thomas volume from his hands, wrapped it in brown paper with quick, decisive folds, and pushed it across the counter. "Eight pounds. He wrote about exactly this. Standing in a place wondering if you've betrayed it. You'll hate how accurate it is." He paid with a twenty and waved off the change toward the charity tin, watching her with an expression she refused to meet. "Eleri Morgan. Would you have dinner with me?" "No." "Coffee?" "No." "Why not?" "Because you ask everyone, and everyone says yes, and I don't fancy being a foregone conclusion." He absorbed this like a man absorbing a line note from a director he respected. "Then I'll come back tomorrow. And buy another book. And the day after. And you can say no until it means something different." "That's the strangest courtship strategy I've ever heard." "I'm improvising. You've thrown out my script." He unlatched the door, then paused, and when he spoke again the vowels had softened, gone valley-deep, just for her: "*Diolch*, Eleri Morgan." The bell jangled. Through the window she watched him square his shoulders, become Rhys Trevelyan again, and stride out into the flashbulbs. She stood there a long moment, then pressed both hands flat against the counter. "Right," she told the empty shop, unconvincingly. "Stock."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The Welsh setting is authentically rendered through place names, cultural references, and dialect, creating a convincing small-town atmosphere. The author successfully establishes the core dynamic between Rhys, a famous actor with a fragile ego, and Eleri, a bookstore owner who appears immune to his charm while being secretly affected by it. The characterization is particularly well-handled. Rhys is portrayed as genuinely charming yet needy, with his vulnerability emerging naturally through his monologue about losing his Welsh accent. This revelation feels earned rather than forced, providing genuine depth to what could have been a stock character. Eleri's resistance to his charm feels authentic - she's not cruel or dismissive, just unimpressed, which makes her eventual softening more believable. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Eleri's dry, practical responses ("Have the sheep done something newsworthy?") contrast well with Rhys's performative charm. The banter feels natural and serves character development rather than existing merely for wit's sake. The prose style is confident and controlled, with effective use of specific details (the convex mirror for shoplifters, the charity tin) that ground the scene in reality. The pacing builds appropriately from the initial comedic setup through the more serious emotional revelation to the tentative romantic possibility at the end. However, the piece occasionally veers toward being overly polished in a way that can feel slightly artificial. Some metaphors and descriptions, while skillful, draw attention to themselves. The ending, while satisfying, resolves perhaps too neatly with Rhys's accent returning and his promise to return daily. The emotional complexity is well-executed, particularly in showing both characters' internal conflicts between attraction and resistance. The chemistry develops believably through their verbal sparring, and the underlying tension is maintained throughout. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Underhold The door swung inward on hinges that had been oiled — recently, and well. That was the first wrong thing. The second was the light: not the guttering tallow stubs the guild favored, but a row of whale-oil lanterns burning steady and bright along the vaulted walls, their brass fittings polished to a surgeon's gleam. Someone had prepared this room. Someone had been *expecting* company. Aya's hand found the cold iron of the doorframe and stopped there. The underhold opened before them like the belly of some great drowned beast — ribbed stone arches slick with harbor-damp, the air thick with salt and lamp-smoke and something older underneath, a mineral reek that Aya's blood recognized before her mind did. The artifact. Close. She could feel it humming against the inside of her skull like a struck tuning fork. And between them and it, seated on a crate of contraband Vask powder with the easy patience of a woman who owned the very dark itself, was Mother Hessaline Crowe. "Shut the door behind you, loves," the guildmistress said. "There's a draught." Aya's stomach dropped through the floor. *She was arrested. The runners took her at dawn. Six witnesses—* "You're meant to be in the Magistrate's iron," Katra said flatly. Her hand had drifted toward her belt-knife and stopped, because four bodyguards had detached themselves from the shadows between the lantern-posts — heavyset men in oilcloth coats, each cradling a brass-throated scattergun of naval pattern. The kind of weapon that didn't require its owner to aim so much as *gesture*. "I'm meant to be a great many things." Crowe rose. She was sixty if a day, grey hair scraped back under a stevedore's cap, and she moved like a woman half that age. A pepperbox pistol hung loose in her hand, all six barrels catching the light. "Arrested, for one. Deposed, for another. It's remarkable what a few coins to the right watchman will purchase. A pantomime of a raid. And then one simply waits, down here in the quiet, to see which of one's dear children comes creeping for the inheritance before the body's even cold." *A trap. The whole night was a trap, and we walked it like sleepwalkers.* Aya's pulse was a hammer against her collar. Behind her, Simeon exhaled slowly through his teeth — his tell, the sound he made when the arithmetic of a situation came up all wrong. "Mother Crowe," he began, palms spreading, "we came to *secure* the hold. Word was the runners were coming back with sappers—" "Simeon Vance, you couldn't lie your way out of a confessional." Crowe flicked two fingers. "Take them." It happened with dreadful, unhurried efficiency. No struggle worth the name — Katra's knife clattered to the flagstones, Simeon's arms were wrenched behind him, and Aya felt hands like dock-cranes close on her shoulders. She reached, reflexively, *inward* — toward the cold black water of the immateria, toward the things that swam there and answered her— A gun barrel pressed beneath her jaw, lifting her chin. "None of that," Crowe said softly, crossing the floor. "Oh yes, I know about you, little shadow. My boys have seen what follows you out of dark corners. Things with too many joints. You so much as *hum* a summoning and Gorrick here paints the ceiling with you, and we'll all take our chances with whatever crawls out of the mess after." She tilted her head, studying the mask. "But first. I do so hate conversing with a face I can't see." *No. No, no—* Fingers hooked the edge of the leather. The mask came away. The silence that followed had weight. One of the bodyguards actually stepped back. "Well," Crowe breathed. A smile spread across her weathered face, slow as oil on water. "Saints in the deep. Lady Aya Veltrane. The Magistrate's own *goddaughter*, slumming in my cellars." She laughed — a dry, delighted rasp. "And here I thought tonight couldn't profit me further." Aya's face was naked to the lamplight and she felt it like a flaying. Twenty years of careful walls, gone in the pull of a strap. Somewhere far above and beyond the guild walls, out in the harbor fog, a foghorn sounded — once, twice, three times — mournful as a funeral bell. *The morning packet steamer,* some detached part of her mind noted. *The world still turning. Ships still sailing. And I am going to die in a cellar that smells of whale-oil and old blood.* Unless. "You're right," Aya said. Her voice came out steadier than she had any right to expect — the aristocrat's voice, the one trained at dinner tables where every sentence was a fencing match. "Profit. Let's discuss it." "The condemned proposes terms. Go on, then. Sing." "You hold the Magistrate's goddaughter. Alive, I'm worth a king's ransom, pardons, leverage over half the Assize bench. Dead, I'm worth a manhunt that ends with this hold burned to the waterline. They hanged my mother for the magick in her blood, Mother Crowe — do you imagine they'll be *gentler* about the men who put a hole in her daughter?" "They hanged your mother," Crowe said, "and they'll hang you the moment they learn what you are. Witchblood and a thief besides. Perhaps I'd be doing the Magistrate a kindness. Sparing the family a *second* scandal." *She's right.* The thought landed cold and complete. The moment her name left this room, Aya Veltrane was finished — the salons, the estate, the protection of her father's grief-bought silence. Lady Aya would die tonight no matter what; the only question was whether the body went with her. *So there is nothing left to protect. Strange, how much that feels like freedom.* "Then you'd be doing it for free," Aya said, "when you could be doing it for a fortune. You didn't stage your own arrest because you're sentimental, Mother Crowe. You did it to count your enemies. So count me instead among your *assets*." Crowe's smile didn't waver, but something moved behind her eyes — the abacus-click of a woman pricing cargo. "Gorrick," she said at last, "fetch chains. The good ones." Her gaze never left Aya's bare face. "And put a kettle on. Her ladyship and I are going to have a long talk about her *value*."
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric worldbuilding within the "whalepunk" fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere through careful attention to industrial details (whale-oil lanterns, brass fittings, scatterguns) and maintains consistent tone throughout. The scene structure follows the prompt requirements effectively, hitting all requested story beats: the trio's entry, the revelation of Crowe's presence, Aya's unmasking and capture, and her attempt to negotiate using her aristocratic identity. The pacing builds tension naturally from the ominous opening details (oiled hinges, unexpected lighting) to the climactic revelation of Aya's true identity. Character voices are well-differentiated and authentic to the setting. Crowe emerges as a compelling antagonist with her calculating pragmatism and street-smart dialogue ("you couldn't lie your way out of a confessional"). Aya's internal voice convincingly shifts from panic to aristocratic composure as she adapts to her exposed situation. The dialogue feels natural and period-appropriate without becoming overly archaic. The prose demonstrates sophisticated craft, particularly in sensory details and metaphorical language. Descriptions like "the underhold opened before them like the belly of some great drowned beast" effectively blend the maritime setting with gothic horror elements. The writing maintains elegance without becoming purple or overwrought. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the "it was all a trap" revelation and the aristocrat-in-disguise trope are familiar territory. The piece also relies heavily on exposition through dialogue in places, though this serves the scene's needs adequately. The emotional complexity is well-handled, particularly Aya's realization that her aristocratic identity is now compromised regardless of survival, leading to her strategic pivot. The ending maintains appropriate darkness without false optimism. Technical execution is strong with natural sentence flow and vivid imagery that serves the story rather than overwhelming it. The piece successfully avoids common amateur pitfalls while delivering an engaging scene that fulfills its narrative purpose.
