+ Iteration 3 — Avg Score: 83.8
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Azra Gambit ## Chapter Three: The Antares Proposition Arthur Neegan tugged at his collar for the eleventh time since departure—he had counted—and reflected that a man who could calculate stress tolerances for a mine shaft three hundred meters beneath the Tharsis regolith ought to be able to manage a simple fastening of formal dress. The collar disagreed. It had been pressed and starched to a rigidity that suggested its makers considered comfort a moral failing. Through the viewport, Mars had shrunk to a rust-colored coin. The transfer vessel *Diplomatic Courier 7* hummed with the steady, reassuring vibration of an ion drive—reassuring, Arthur thought sourly, only if you didn't know that somewhere in its engine housing sat a sliver of Azra no larger than a thumbnail, gnawing at the half-lives of its fuel rods like a patient rat. His Azra. Or rather, Azra very much like the vein that ran beneath his unassuming forty hectares, and which had transformed him in nine days from an obscure mining engineer into a man worth abducting politely. It had been polite. That was the strange part. The retinue that had descended on his homestead—six men in pressure suits of diplomatic gray, the four-pointed star of Antares stitched in silver thread at the shoulder—had wiped their boots at the door. Their captain had inquired after his health. They had given him an hour to pack, suggested the formal wear with the gentle implacability of a glacier, and never once displayed the sidearms that nonetheless sat very visibly at their hips. *Diplomatic corps,* Arthur thought. *Diplomatic the way a sandstorm is a breeze.* The hatch at the cabin's forward end sighed open. A young officer inclined his head. "Mr. Neegan. The Envoy will see you now." --- The stateroom beyond was an anachronism in flight—paneled in what appeared to be genuine Earth walnut, lit by lamps of warm amber rather than the blue-white glare standard on Martian installations. Behind a broad desk sat a woman of perhaps sixty, silver-haired, with the kind of stillness that suggested vast reserves of patience or vast reserves of power. In Arthur's experience the two were difficult to distinguish. "Mr. Neegan. I am Envoy Caterina Voss. Sit down, please. The chair nearest the viewport—I'm told the view is wasted on me, but it needn't be wasted on you." Arthur sat. "You'll forgive me, Envoy, if I skip the pleasantries. I was invited here by six armed men. I'd like to know why." "Armed men." She smiled faintly. "Were you threatened?" "No." "Detained?" "Not in so many words." "Then let us call it what it was: an urgent invitation, urgently delivered. Mr. Neegan, do you know how many independent claims on Mars contain workable Azra deposits?" "I'd guess fewer than a dozen." "Three. Yours is the largest by an order of magnitude. The other two are held by men who sold their mineral rights to the Consolidated Outworld Combine within a week of discovery. One of them now lives quite comfortably on Ceres. The other"—she paused with the precision of a metronome—"suffered a regrettable pressure-seal failure before the paperwork cleared. The Combine acquired his claim through probate, at considerable savings." Arthur's collar felt tighter still. "You're telling me I'm in danger." "I'm telling you that you are *valuable*, Mr. Neegan, which on Mars is much the same thing. Consider the larger arithmetic. A gram of Azra, properly configured, can compress the decay of a fission pile from millennia into minutes. That makes starships practical. It also makes a warhead small enough to fit in a valise capable of rendering a city—or a planetary administration—into a historical footnote. Every government, every combine, every navy between here and Antares understands this. The metal that gives mankind the stars also gives him a knife at his own throat. And you, a first-generation settler with calluses on his hands, are sitting on more of it than the Terran Defense Reserve." "And Antares wants it." "Antares wants *stability*." Voss folded her hands. "The Combine would strip your vein in five years and sell to the highest bidder—and the highest bidders for weapons-grade Azra are not pleasant people. The Terran authority would nationalize it and ignite a colonial revolt; you've seen the indenture camps, you know how thin Martian patience runs. We propose a third arrangement." "Which is?" "You retain title. Full title, Mr. Neegan—registered with the Outer Courts, beyond the Combine's reach. Antares takes a minority partnership: we provide security, refining infrastructure, and a price floor triple the Combine's standing offer. Extraction proceeds at a rate *we* determine—slowly. The vein becomes a regulated tap rather than a gusher. No one power obtains a decisive stockpile, and the balance holds." Arthur was quiet a moment. Beyond the viewport, the stars sat in their cold and ancient configurations, indifferent to half-lives and treaties alike. "That's a remarkably reasonable offer," he said slowly, "from people who showed up with sidearms." "The sidearms were not for you." Voss's voice did not change, which somehow made it worse. "Your homestead has been under surveillance by at least two other parties for six days. We merely arrived first, and in sufficient strength to discourage interruption." "And if I refuse?" "Then you will be returned home, unharmed, with our compliments. And within a month you will accept a worse offer from someone less courteous—or your heirs will. I do not say this as a threat, Mr. Neegan. I say it as an engineer might: a statement of load and tolerance." Arthur looked at his hands—miner's hands, ill-suited to walnut desks and starched collars. "I'll want it in writing. Reviewed by my own counsel. On Mars." "Naturally." Voss rose and extended her hand. "One more thing. When you return, you may be contacted by a man calling himself Doran, claiming to represent the settlers' cooperative. He represents nothing of the kind. Hear him out, by all means. But count your fingers afterward." Arthur shook her hand and wondered, not for the first time that day, whether he had just been rescued or simply purchased at a better price. The collar, he decided, was the least of his discomforts.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and successfully captures many elements of Asimov's style. The author effectively establishes the retrofuturistic atmosphere through details like ion drives, pressure suits, and colonial Mars settings. The world-building operates on both macro and micro levels - from the galactic implications of Azra as a game-changing resource to the intimate detail of Arthur counting his collar adjustments. The dialogue carries the narrative effectively, with Envoy Voss serving as an exposition vehicle while maintaining character authenticity. Her measured, diplomatic speech patterns contrast well with Arthur's more direct, working-class perspective. The conversation reveals plot information naturally while building tension about Arthur's precarious position. The prose style successfully evokes classic science fiction without feeling pastiche. Phrases like "gnawing at the half-lives of its fuel rods like a patient rat" and "diplomatic the way a sandstorm is a breeze" demonstrate creative metaphorical thinking. The writing maintains consistent tone throughout, balancing technical detail with human emotion. However, the piece suffers from some predictability in its central premise - the "small man caught between powerful forces" trope is well-worn in science fiction. While executed competently, it doesn't offer surprising twists on familiar territory. The political machinations, while clearly established, follow expected patterns of corporate vs. governmental interests. The characterization of Arthur feels authentic but somewhat limited. His reactions are believable but not particularly complex - he responds as expected for someone in his situation. Voss is more intriguing as a character, though she remains somewhat archetypal as the smooth diplomatic manipulator. The pacing works well, building from Arthur's discomfort through the revelation of stakes to the final ambiguous agreement. The ending appropriately maintains uncertainty about whether Arthur has been helped or exploited, which fits the noir-influenced tone. Technical execution is strong throughout, with natural sentence flow and effective scene-setting. The piece fulfills its assignment requirements comprehensively while maintaining readability and engagement. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 14 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 82.0
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Darkroom Revelations The red light makes everything look like the inside of a heart. That's what I'm thinking as I watch Elliot's hands move through the developer tray, his black-painted nails catching the safelight like beetle shells. He's wearing that oversized mesh top again, the one layered over a tank from some band that broke up before he was born, and silver rings on four fingers. I've spent the whole semester cataloguing these details like evidence of something I haven't named yet. "You're staring," he says, not looking up. His voice is soft but not weak—there's a difference I've been learning all semester. "I'm supervising. You're going to over-develop that." He pulls the print with the tongs, exactly on time, and gives me this look through his dark fringe. Quietly smug. God. Here's the thing about me: I take charge. I've dated guys who liked being told where to stand and women who appreciated that I always made the first move, and I've never apologized for any of it. My ex called me "a lot," which I took as a compliment. But around Elliot, my usual machinery jams. I find myself softening my voice, asking questions instead of making statements, and I don't fully understand it. We hang the print and stand back. It's one of his—a self-portrait, double-exposed, his face overlapping with a blur of fabric and light. He's been making a lot of these lately. Faces that won't resolve. "Can I tell you something weird?" he says. "My favorite genre." He laughs, then doesn't say anything for long enough that I have to physically restrain myself from filling the silence. Give him room, I keep telling myself. Don't bulldoze. This is a person who needs space to arrive at things, and you, Dani, are a human steamroller. "I've been thinking about gender," he says finally. "Like. Mine. Whether 'boy' is actually... the whole thing. Or any of the thing." The developer fumes sting my nose. My phone buzzes in my pocket—probably the group chat, probably nothing—and I let it die there. This moment feels like a print mid-development: touch it wrong and you ruin it. "Okay," I say carefully. "Tell me more, if you want." "That's it, kind of. That's the whole confession." He gestures at the hanging prints, all those layered, dissolving faces. "I've been making it all semester apparently. My therapist would call that 'externalizing.' I call it being too chicken to say it out loud until now." He pauses. "You're the first person I've told." Something in my chest does a complicated thing. Pride that he chose me. Tenderness so sharp it's almost painful. And underneath it, a low hum I have to be honest about: attraction, getting stronger, getting more confusing. Because here's my own confession, the one I'm not making out loud: I've always known my type. Defined edges. Butch women, masculine men, people who'd planted their flags. I liked clarity. And now I'm standing in red light watching this boy—this person—come beautifully unfixed, and I'm more drawn to him than I've been to anyone in years. What does that make me? Someone whose categories were always flimsier than she thought, maybe. The thought is vertiginous and not entirely unpleasant. "How does it feel?" I ask. "Saying it." "Terrifying. Good. Like when you watch the image come up in the tray and you don't know yet if you exposed it right." He fidgets with one of his rings. "Sometimes I want to try things. Different name, maybe. Makeup beyond eyeliner. But then I think, what if I try it and it's wrong, or worse, what if it's right and everything changes—" "Hey." I step closer, then catch myself. Every instinct I have says *take his hands, tell him exactly what to do, fix it.* I override all of them. "You don't have to know. You're allowed to develop slowly." I wince. "Sorry. Darkroom metaphors. Occupational hazard." But he's smiling, really smiling, and in the red light his face looks open in a way I've never seen it. "I thought you'd—I don't know. You're so sure of everything. I thought uncertainty might annoy you." "I'm not sure of everything." It comes out quieter than I intend. "I'm not even sure why I volunteered to partner with you when Marcus asked me first." The silence after that is loud. The timer ticks. Somewhere down the hall a door closes. "Why did you?" he asks. I could deflect. Instead I choose honesty, which feels like its own kind of darkroom—working blind, trusting the chemistry. "Because I've been paying attention to you all semester. Your weird thrift-store armor. The way you actually think before you talk. I liked you before tonight, and tonight you told me something true about yourself, and now I like you more, and I'm trying very hard not to make this moment about me." He's quiet. Then: "You can make it a little about you." "Okay. Then—this is new for me too. Liking someone who's mid-question instead of mid-answer. It's rearranging some furniture in my head. But I want you to know the rearranging feels good. You're not a complication, Elliot. You're—" I gesture at the prints, the overlapping luminous faces. "You're the most interesting thing in this room, and this room is full of art." He ducks his head, and even in red light I can tell he's blushing. Then he does something brave: he reaches out and takes my hand. His rings are cold; his fingers aren't. "I might not be Elliot forever," he says. "The name, I mean." "Then I'll learn the new one." I squeeze his hand. "First try." We stand there under the safelight, not kissing, not yet—just holding on while the prints drip-dry around us like flags of some country neither of us has maps for. My phone buzzes again, insistent, irrelevant. The whole world can wait. Something's still developing in here.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive art student whose usual confidence becomes complicated by her attraction to Elliot and her desire to respect his vulnerability during his gender questioning journey. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, with the red light creating an intimate confessional atmosphere while photography terminology ("developing slowly," "exposed it right") provides organic metaphors for identity exploration. The author shows restraint in not over-extending these metaphors while making them feel natural to the characters and setting. Dani's characterization is particularly well-executed. Her internal struggle between her natural take-charge personality and her conscious effort to give Elliot space feels authentic and creates genuine tension. The line "Give him room, I keep telling myself. Don't bulldoze. This is a person who needs space to arrive at things, and you, Dani, are a human steamroller" effectively captures this internal conflict while maintaining her voice. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, avoiding both overly clinical discussions of gender identity and unrealistic profound statements. Elliot's tentative revelation ("I've been thinking about gender... Whether 'boy' is actually... the whole thing") captures the hesitancy and uncertainty of someone beginning to explore their identity. The piece handles the complexity of Dani's attraction thoughtfully, acknowledging her confusion about being drawn to someone whose gender presentation challenges her usual preferences. This adds psychological depth without making the story primarily about her rather than Elliot's revelation. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension through small moments and allowing silence to carry weight. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful without being unrealistically resolved, acknowledging the uncertainty ahead while establishing emotional connection. Some minor weaknesses include occasional phrases that border on precious ("vertiginous and not entirely unpleasant") and the metaphor of uncertainty as darkroom development, while apt, is perhaps slightly over-relied upon. The piece also could have pushed deeper into Dani's identity questioning, though this may be appropriate given the word limit and focus. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 85.5
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Stolen Moments in Roma **I. The Trevi Fountain, 9:47 AM** The Vespa missed him by the width of a guidebook—specifically, the one Ben Whitmore was holding in front of his face like a shield against the entire concept of Italy. "*Ma sei scemo?*" The rider yanked off her helmet, releasing a cascade of dark curls and a torrent of Italian that Ben understood approximately none of, though the tone translated universally. "I'm terribly sorry—*mi dispiace*—I was just reading about the fountain's baroque—" "You read about the fountain while standing in the street with your back to the fountain." She looked him up and down: tweed jacket in June, sensible shoes, the pallor of a man who'd spent his life under library lighting. "*Inglese*." "Is it that obvious?" "You apologized to my motorcycle." She swung off the bike, and Ben noticed she was laughing at him, but somehow not unkindly. "Alessandra." "Ben. Benjamin. Ben's fine." "Benjamin-Ben's-Fine. Throw a coin in the fountain. It's the law." He fumbled for change. She watched him toss it with the athletic grace of a man hurling a wet sock, and made a decision that would derail his entire day. "You're doing Rome wrong," she announced. "Get on the bike." --- **II. Somewhere in Trastevere, 11:20 AM** Ben had never traveled at such speeds in his life, including on airplanes, which at least had the decency to feel slow. He'd clutched Alessandra's jacket the whole way while she narrated landmarks at eighty kilometers an hour: "*Pantheon. Two thousand years old. Hold on.*" Now they sat at a café table that Alessandra had simply claimed, despite the *Riservato* sign, which she'd flipped face-down with two fingers. "That was reserved," Ben said. "For us. Clearly." She stole a sip of his espresso before he'd touched it. "So. Sabbatical. You study what?" "Medieval manuscripts. Marginalia, specifically. The little drawings monks made in the margins. Rabbits with swords, that sort of thing." Alessandra stared at him, then laughed—a real one, head thrown back. "Monks doodling. This is your *life*." "It's more interesting than it sounds." "It would have to be." When they left, Ben noticed a sugar bowl from the table had somehow migrated into her jacket pocket. He decided he'd imagined it. --- **III. The Mercato di Campo de' Fiori, 1:15 PM** He had not imagined it. He watched her drift through the market like weather—charming a vendor in dialect, arguing about peaches, and somewhere in the choreography of it, a single fig disappeared from a crate and reappeared, halved, in her palm. "You didn't pay for that," he whispered, scandalized. "Marco overcharges tourists. I'm restoring balance to the universe." She pressed half the fig into his hand. "Eat your stolen goods, professor." He should have refused. He ate it. It was the best fig of his life, and he suspected the larceny was a contributing factor. "You're corrupting me," he said. "You walked into traffic reading a book. You were already lost. I'm just choosing the direction." --- **IV. A Rooftop That Was Definitely Not Open to the Public, 3:30 PM** "This is trespassing." "This is the best view in Rome." Alessandra had talked them past a doorman with a story Ben didn't follow but which involved a great deal of hand-waving and possibly a fictional uncle. Now the city spread below them, terracotta and gold, domes rising like held breaths. Ben forgot to be nervous. "Oh," he said quietly. "*Oh.*" She watched him instead of the view. "There he is." "Who?" "The person under the tweed." She leaned on the railing beside him, close enough that he became acutely aware of the six centimeters between their shoulders. "Tell me something true, Benjamin-Ben's-Fine. Something you've never told anyone." He considered. "I came to Rome because my life had become a margin. All annotation. No text." Alessandra was quiet for a moment—the first stillness he'd seen in her. "That's almost poetry." "And you? Something true?" She smiled, and it was the smile of a locked door. "I only steal things nobody will miss." A beat. "Usually." He didn't ask what she meant. He was afraid the answer was *afternoons*. Or possibly *Englishmen*. --- **V. Via del Governo Vecchio, 6:50 PM** The sky cracked open without warning—a proper Roman downpour, theatrical and absolute. Alessandra grabbed his hand and ran, and they crashed laughing under the striped awning of a gelateria, soaked through, pressed shoulder to shoulder as rain hammered the cobblestones into mirrors. "Your tweed," she said, "smells like a wet historian." "Your hair is dripping on me." "You're welcome." She bought two gelatos—actually bought them, he noted, watching her count out coins with exaggerated ceremony, eyes on him the whole time. *See? I'm capable of it.* Pistachio for her, stracciatella for him, because she ordered without asking and was infuriatingly correct. They stood under the awning eating gelato in the rain, and Ben thought: *I will remember this exact minute for the rest of my life.* "Tomorrow," Alessandra said, not looking at him, "I work until two." "Is that—are you telling me your schedule?" "I'm telling you the Trevi Fountain at three. Don't read while standing in traffic. I won't always be there to nearly kill you." "That's the most romantic threat I've ever received." She turned, and the mischief in her eyes softened into something briefer, realer, gone before he could name it. Then she kissed his cheek—quick, light, a theft in its own right—and stepped backward into the easing rain toward her Vespa. "*Ciao*, Benjamin." It wasn't until she'd roared off around the corner that Ben reached into his jacket pocket and found his fountain pen missing—and in its place, a coin. The same one, he was almost certain, he'd thrown into the Trevi that morning. A guarantee of return, the legend said. Ben stood under the awning, soaked and grinning like a fool, holding his own wish in his hand.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in crafting a contemporary romance set in Rome. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge naturally through dialogue and action rather than exposition. Alessandra's mischievous energy and Ben's scholarly awkwardness are well-contrasted, and their chemistry develops believably over the course of a single day. The structure works effectively, with five interconnected scenes that build momentum and deepen the relationship. The writing style is confident and polished, with particularly strong dialogue that captures both characters' voices authentically. Alessandra's rapid-fire wit and Ben's flustered politeness feel genuine, and their conversations crackle with tension and humor. The author handles the cultural clash element skillfully, avoiding stereotypes while still highlighting the differences between the characters' backgrounds and temperaments. The kleptomania subplot is woven in subtly and serves both character development and plot advancement, culminating in the clever pen/coin exchange that provides satisfying closure. The prose quality is consistently high, with vivid sensory details that bring Rome to life without overwhelming the narrative. Descriptions like "terracotta and gold, domes rising like held breaths" are evocative without being overwrought. The pacing is excellent, moving between moments of action, dialogue, and reflection. The emotional development feels earned rather than rushed. Ben's transformation from rigid academic to willing accomplice happens gradually through specific incidents that challenge his comfort zone. The romantic tension builds naturally without forced moments or unbelievable declarations. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the cleverness of the prose draws slight attention to itself, and a few descriptions that edge toward being overly polished. However, these are minor quibbles in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully balances humor, romance, and character development within the constraints of the prompt. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter One: Vega Whisky, Neat The gravity hiccups again as I'm polishing the last of the night's glassware, and I watch the amber surface of old Hesper's drink ripple like a pond someone's skipped a stone across. Third fluctuation this shift. Maintenance keeps promising they'll recalibrate the plates, and I keep promising patrons their drinks won't float away. We're both liars. "Nets, sweetheart, you're hovering," Hesper grumbles, steadying his glass with one weathered hand. He's been mining iridium out here since before I was born, allegedly. "I'm protecting your investment. That's twelve-credit whisky wobbling around in there." "It's nine-credit whisky and you know it." "Inflation, Hesp." He snorts, and I drift down the bar, checking on the usual evening crowd. The Outpost hums with its familiar music: the click of dice from the corner table where the Okonkwo twins are fleecing some freighter crew, the low burble of the algae tanks behind the bar, the distant groan of docking clamps somewhere above us. Mira's flirting with a cargo inspector by the viewport, the asteroid field rotating slow and silver behind them. Home. Or close enough that I've stopped checking the difference. That's when she comes in. The door cycles and a figure slips through — tall, wrapped in a traveling cloak that's seen better decades, tentacled dreads askew like she's been running her hands through them for days. Or running, full stop. She crosses the floor with the careful, too-smooth gait of someone trying very hard to look casual, and quietly plops down at the bar two stools from Hesper. "Vega whisky, neat." Her voice is sandpaper and exhaustion. "Better make that a double." "Coming up." I pull the bottle — the real Vega, not the Proxima knockoff I serve the tourists — and pour. The gravity flutters and the whisky sways in the glass like it's nervous too. "Rough transit?" "You could say that." She takes the glass in both hands. One of her dreads twitches, curling slightly, tasting the air the way her people do when they're scanning for threats. I tended bar on Kellis Station long enough to know a Vethari stress response when I see one. "First time at the Outpost?" "Last time, hopefully. No offense." "None taken. We're nobody's destination. That's the charm." She almost smiles. Almost. Then the door cycles again and her whole body goes rigid — but it's just Petrov, coming off shift, stomping in with his usual demand for beer and grievances. She exhales. Drinks half the double in one pull. "You waiting for someone?" I ask, keeping my voice down, keeping my hands busy with a towel. "Avoiding someone. There's a difference." "Not always, in my experience." Her dreads still. She looks at me properly for the first time — eyes the color of deep water, ringed with sleeplessness. "What's your name?" "Nettle. Nets, if you stay long enough to earn it." "Saya." A pause, like she's deciding whether the name is safe to give. "How fast does news travel out here, Nets?" "Depends on the news. Mining quotas, fast. Gossip, faster. Anything official from the Core?" I shrug. "Slow. Relay's down half the time. Why?" "No reason." Which is what people say when there's exactly one reason and it's a bad one. She finishes the whisky. "Another." I pour. The gravity dips and we both watch the liquid bulge weirdly upward for a half-second before settling. "Sorry. Station's got hiccups tonight." "It's fine. I like it here. It's..." She searches for the word. "Forgettable." "We do our best." Hesper, who has the social radar of a cargo drone, leans over. "You come in on that courier ship? The one that docked at bay six with no transponder ID?" Saya's hand tightens on the glass. "Lots of ships run quiet out here." "Sure, sure. Just unusual, is all. Quiet ship, quiet pilot." He raises his drink amiably. "Quiet's underrated." "Hesp," I say, "your glass is empty and your mouth isn't. Let me fix one of those problems." He chuckles and slides his glass over, but the damage is done. Saya's already pulling credits from inside her cloak — physical chits, untraceable, which tells me plenty — and stacking them on the bar. "You don't have to run," I hear myself say, quietly. "Hesper's harmless. Memory like a sieve, anyway." "It's not him I'm worried about." She hesitates. Then, lower: "Nets. The transponder logs at bay six. Who has access?" "Dockmaster. Station admin. Anyone admin owes a favor to." I study her. "What's going to come looking for you, Saya?" "Not what. Who." Her dreads coil tight against her skull. "And if they get here before I can fix my drive coupling, this forgettable little station is going to become very memorable, very fast." The honest thing, the smart thing — the thing the old me would have done — is to slide her credits back, wish her luck, and forget her face by closing time. I came to the Outpost to be done with other people's emergencies. I have my own past breathing down my neck; I don't need to borrow anyone else's. The gravity flutters. Every glass on the bar shivers in sympathy. "How long to fix a drive coupling?" I ask. "With the parts I have? Two days. Maybe three." "And how long before your who shows up?" "If I'm lucky? Two days. Maybe three." I pour her a third whisky without being asked, and one for myself, which I never do on shift. The liquid trembles in both glasses, unsteady, waiting. "Drink up," I say. "Then tell me what kind of trouble we're in." Her eyes narrow. "We?" "You docked at my station and ordered my good whisky. That's practically a contract." Across the room, the door cycles. Saya doesn't turn around. But every one of her dreads does.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in establishing a cozy sci-fi atmosphere while building tension effectively. The author successfully creates the requested bar setting with vivid details like gravity fluctuations, algae tanks, and the diverse cast of regulars. The world-building feels lived-in and authentic, from the mining operations to the station's technical problems. The dialogue flows naturally and carries much of the narrative weight as requested (approximately 60% dialogue). Conversations feel authentic, particularly the banter between Nets and Hesper, and the cautious exchange between Nets and Saya. The author skillfully uses dialogue to reveal character relationships and advance the plot without exposition dumps. Character work is particularly strong. Nets emerges as a compelling protagonist with a clear voice - pragmatic but caring, experienced but not cynical. Her decision to help Saya feels earned through the gradual revelation of her protective instincts. Saya is effectively mysterious without being opaque, and her alien physiology (tentacled dreads that react to stress) adds texture to her characterization. The conflict setup is well-executed. The author establishes Saya's desperation, the time pressure of repairs versus pursuit, and raises stakes for the entire station. The ending creates genuine tension without premature resolution, exactly as requested. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with effective sensory details like the whisky rippling during gravity fluctuations and the station's ambient sounds. The writing avoids purple prose while still being evocative. The pacing builds steadily from mundane bar operations to mounting tension. Minor weaknesses include some predictable elements (mysterious stranger with pursuers is a familiar trope) and occasional moments where the dialogue carries slightly more exposition than feels completely natural. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece successfully establishes the cozy sci-fi tone while introducing genuine stakes and conflict. It adheres closely to all prompt requirements and creates a compelling opening chapter that leaves readers wanting more. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 84.1
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gone South — Chapter Two: Twelve Percent The thing nobody tells you about running away is that it's mostly just standing around. I'm standing on a corner of Canal Street at 7 a.m., backpack straps cutting into my shoulders, watching a guy in a sequined jacket hose vomit off the sidewalk like it's the most normal thing in the world. Maybe here it is. Behind me, a streetcar groans past, packed with people who all have somewhere to be. I have nowhere to be. That fact keeps hitting me in waves — terrifying, then exhilarating, then terrifying again, like a song with two choruses fighting each other. My phone says 12%. It also says three missed calls from Mom and a text from my brother that just reads: *seriously?* Seriously, Caleb. Seriously. I don't answer. I can't. If I hear Mom's voice — even her angry voice, even the voice she used two nights ago when she said *I don't know who you are anymore* — I might fold. And I didn't ride four hundred miles in a long-haul trucker's cab listening to talk radio about the end times just to fold. The trucker, for the record, had been fine. Boring, even. Dropped me downtown with a "watch yourself, kid" and drove off. I'd braced the whole ride for something terrible, hand on the door handle, and the worst thing that happened was the talk radio. I'm learning that danger doesn't announce itself the way you expect. That's the scary part. I find a bench in a park by the river — the Mississippi, fat and brown and moving like it's got somewhere to be too — and I do inventory. Forty-three dollars. Half a sleeve of crackers. A hoodie. A phone at 12% with no charger because of course I forgot the charger, because I packed in a panic at midnight while Dad's words were still ringing through the wall. *Not under my roof.* Cool, Dad. I found a different roof. It's the sky. It's huge. "You're doing the math face." I look up. A kid — older than me, maybe seventeen, eighteen — has dropped onto the other end of my bench like we planned to meet here. Dark curly hair, a denim jacket covered in safety pins, a paper bag of beignets that smells like heaven would smell if heaven were fried. "The what?" "The math face. Money divided by days, carry the panic." They hold out the bag. "Beignet? They gave me extra." Every stranger-danger lecture I've ever received fires off at once. But also: powdered sugar. I take one. "I'm Rue," they say. "Like the regret, not the street. Although also like the street, I guess. This city's confusing." "Taylor." It comes out easy. My name. *My* name, two days old and already fitting better than the old one ever did. Rue just nods, no follow-up questions, and something in my chest unclenches half an inch. "How long you been out?" Rue asks. "Out?" "Out *here.* On your own." A beat. "Though the other kind of out, too, if you want. You've got the look." "What look?" "Like you just told the truth and the truth set you free and then the truth also made you homeless." Rue says it lightly, but their eyes aren't light at all. "Eleven months for me. The being-out-here part." Eleven months. I do new math, worse math. "Where do you — like, where do you sleep?" "Depends. There's a drop-in center on Rampart, does showers and phone charging—" my head snaps up and Rue laughs, "—yeah, I figured. Everybody's phone is always dying. It's like a law." We walk. Rue narrates the city like a tour guide who's been mugged by the tour: which blocks are fine, which guys offering "work" you walk away from fast, which church does dinner on Wednesdays without making you pray for it. I take mental notes like there's going to be a test, because there is, the test is every single day apparently. "Why eleven months?" I ask, when we stop at a light. "Why not — I don't know. Go back?" Rue's face does something complicated, a door closing behind a curtain. "There's nothing to go back to." Then, brighter, fake-brighter: "Light's green." I don't push. But I notice they checked over their shoulder twice in three blocks, and not in a casual way. I notice they paid for those beignets with a crumpled twenty from a roll of crumpled twenties, which is a weird amount of cash for someone sleeping in drop-in centers. I notice, and I file it away, and I hate that I'm already learning to do that — to catalog people, weigh them, hold back. At the drop-in center, a woman named Denise gets my phone on a charger and slides me a granola bar and a card with a hotline number, and doesn't ask anything I can't answer. The phone climbs to 19%, 24%. Mom calls again while it's charging. I watch her name pulse on the screen and my throat does this awful tightening thing. "You don't have to answer," Rue says quietly, beside me. "But — answer eventually. If there's anyone worth answering. Trust me on that one." The call goes to voicemail. I stare at the screen. Then I type, before I can think: *I'm safe. I'm not coming back until you can use my name. It's Taylor.* Send. 31%. Outside, the afternoon's gone gold and humid, and somewhere down the block a trumpet is playing something that sounds like crying and laughing at the same time, and Rue is saying there's a place we can probably crash that's "mostly safe, like seventy percent," and I am sixteen years old and four hundred miles from everything and I have forty-one dollars and a stranger with secrets and a phone at 31%. It's not enough. It's nowhere near enough. But it's mine. All of it — the fear, the river, the name. Mine. I shoulder my backpack and follow the trumpet.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in depicting a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The author successfully captures Taylor's voice through a compelling blend of vulnerability, defiance, and dark humor that feels genuinely adolescent without being patronizing. The adherence to instructions is excellent - the piece follows Taylor through their first day, introduces another runaway (Rue) with clear secrets, maintains first-person mixed tense throughout, and incorporates substantial internal monologue alongside natural dialogue. The 1000-word target is met appropriately. Character development is particularly strong. Taylor's internal contradictions feel authentic - the simultaneous terror and exhilaration of freedom, the bravado masking fear, the careful cataloging of survival information while mourning lost innocence. Rue is efficiently sketched as a compelling secondary character with clear depth suggested through selective details (the roll of twenties, checking over shoulders, the eleven-month timeline with "nothing to go back to"). The prose demonstrates sophisticated control, particularly in the opening hook about running away being "mostly just standing around" and recurring motifs like the phone battery percentage serving as both practical concern and metaphor for dwindling resources. The author effectively uses concrete details (sequined jacket, vomit-hosing, talk radio about end times) to ground the narrative in specific place and moment. Dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - establishing character, advancing plot, and revealing backstory efficiently. Rue's "Like the regret, not the street" introduction and their observation about Taylor's "math face" demonstrate personality while moving the story forward. The emotional complexity is well-handled, avoiding both excessive sentimentality and unrealistic toughness. Taylor's conflicted feelings about family contact, the careful dance of trust with Rue, and the mixture of fear and empowerment in their situation all ring true. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphorical language feels slightly forced ("like a song with two choruses fighting each other") and the ending, while thematically appropriate, edges toward being overly neat in its affirmation. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece succeeds in creating genuine tension about Taylor's situation while maintaining hope, and establishes clear stakes for future chapters without resolving everything tidily. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 6 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 85.9
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# My Apocalypse Buddy The end of the world, as it turned out, had not been particularly dramatic. Civilization hadn't gone out with a bang so much as a prolonged administrative error, and what remained was mostly rust, weeds, and people like Midas, who survived through a combination of luck, optimism, and a profound inability to learn from experience. "The way I see it," said Midas, peering over the lip of the water tower they called home, "is that we need batteries, and the Mall of Reasonable Prices has batteries." "The way *I* see it," said Chewy, "is that the Mall of Reasonable Prices also has the Cutlery Boys, who have made it abundantly clear that your continued existence is a personal affront to them." Chewy was a stuffed koala, approximately forty centimeters of matted grey plush wrapped around servo motors and a personality chip that some long-dead engineer had calibrated, presumably as a joke, to *sardonic*. His eyes—originally designed to glow a comforting amber during bedtime stories—now flickered through the spectrum according to his moods, which was inconvenient for a creature whose survival strategy relied heavily on pretending to be inanimate. They were currently flickering a skeptical puce. "That was one time," said Midas. "You traded their leader a tin of peaches that turned out to be dog food." "The label was faded." "You *licked the lid* and told him it was peaches." "I have a confident palate." Midas shouldered his pack, which contained a rope, half a rope, a knife that was mostly handle, and a map he'd drawn himself from memory, which is to say it was a work of speculative fiction. "Besides, your battery light's been blinking red for a week. You want to power down mid-sentence and spend eternity as a very judgmental cushion?" This was, Chewy had to admit, a compelling argument. He climbed into the pack's top pouch, ears protruding like a periscope with opinions. --- The journey to the Mall took them through the Whispering Underpass (so named because of the wind, and also the hermit who lived there and narrated passersby in an unsettling murmur: *"...and lo, the fool returneth, bearing his cursed bear..."* "Koala," Chewy corrected, as he always did, and the hermit, as he always did, pretended not to hear). The Mall of Reasonable Prices rose from the scrubland like a cathedral built by people who worshipped parking. Midas crouched behind a fossilized shopping trolley and surveyed the entrance. "No guards," he whispered. "We're in luck." "We are never in luck," said Chewy. "We are occasionally in luck-adjacent circumstances which then collapse. Your eyes, incidentally, should be checked, because there are *two* guards, by the pretzel kiosk." There were. Midas, employing what he generously called stealth, circled to the loading dock, pried open a door with the knife-handle, and crept into the dim cavern of dead commerce. Electronics 'R' Everything sat at the far end, past a fountain now occupied by something that croaked territorially. The batteries were exactly where batteries always were: locked in a cage designed to thwart shoplifters and, posthumously, survivors. Midas began working the lock with a bent fork he kept for such occasions. "Someone's coming," hissed Chewy, eyes strobing alarm-orange and thereby illuminating their hiding spot like a small festive emergency. "Turn your eyes *off*." "They're emotionally autonomous! You think I *enjoy* broadcasting?" The someone was Grevil, leader of the Cutlery Boys, a man who had filed his name down to match his teeth. He stood at the end of the aisle, machete gleaming, gaze fixed on the disco-pulse of Chewy's panic. "Peaches," he said, with the warmth of a meat locker. "Grevil!" said Midas. "Funny story about that tin—" What followed was less a fight than a pursuit, and less a pursuit than a guided tour of the Mall's structural weaknesses. Midas vaulted the fountain (the croaking thing got a mouthful of trouser), skidded through Scented Candle Emporium (releasing a forty-year-old cloud of Vanilla Dream that incapacitated one pursuer outright), and found himself cornered in Soft Furnishings. "Throw me," said Chewy. "What?" "I'm a stuffed animal among five hundred stuffed animals. Throw me at the display, grab batteries in the confusion, and *come back for me*. I cannot stress that last clause enough." Midas threw him. Chewy sailed into a pyramid of plush rabbits, eyes clamped grimly dark. Grevil rounded the corner to find one unarmed scavenger and an avalanche of toys, one of which he stepped on, which *squeaked*, which made him look down, which gave Midas time to swing a decorative lamp with the desperation of a man who has run entirely out of plans. It connected. Grevil sat down among the rabbits, contemplating new constellations. Midas grabbed two packs of batteries, then spent eleven harrowing minutes failing to identify Chewy among the plush ("It's *me*, you absolute parsnip—" "They all look like you!" "*None* of them look like me, I have *gravitas*") before the remaining Cutlery Boys arrived and the exit strategy became less a strategy than a velocity. --- They came home through the Underpass ("...*and the fool returneth again, smelling of vanilla and regret...*"), Midas limping, one trouser leg surrendered to the fountain creature, Chewy missing an ear and riding in the pack with the affronted dignity of damaged royalty. At the water tower, Midas swapped in the new batteries. Chewy's eyes brightened to a steady, healthy amber—then flickered, briefly, an embarrassed pink. "What's that one?" asked Midas. "Nothing." "That's the gratitude color, isn't it." "It's a voltage irregularity." "You're *welcome*, Chewy." "You threw me into rabbits." "You *asked* me to." "And you came back," said Chewy, very quietly, eyes pink as a sunset. "Eleven minutes late, smelling like a candle shop. But you came back." Outside, the world continued ending, slowly, administratively. Inside, two survivors split a tin of probably-peaches and agreed, as they always did, that tomorrow they'd be more careful. They wouldn't be.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, successfully capturing his dry wit, observational humor, and humanistic warmth. The author effectively employs Pratchett's characteristic technique of finding profound meaning in mundane details, as seen in the opening line about civilization ending through "administrative error" rather than dramatic catastrophe. The characterization is particularly well-executed. Midas emerges as a believable post-apocalyptic everyman whose optimism and poor judgment feel authentic rather than forced. Chewy, the animatronic koala, is brilliantly conceived - his sardonic personality chip and color-changing eyes provide both humor and genuine pathos. The dynamic between them feels lived-in and genuine, with their banter revealing deep familiarity and underlying affection. The prose maintains Pratchett's signature balance of clever wordplay and accessible storytelling. Phrases like "a knife that was mostly handle" and "a map he'd drawn himself from memory, which is to say it was a work of speculative fiction" demonstrate the author's command of Pratchett's observational humor. The world-building is economical but vivid, creating a believable post-apocalyptic landscape through selective details. The story structure follows the requested format perfectly: deliberation, mission gone wrong, return home worse for wear. The Mall sequence provides genuine tension while maintaining the comedic tone, and the various obstacles (Cutlery Boys, fountain creature, vanilla cloud) feel organic to this world rather than contrived. The dialogue sparkles throughout, particularly Chewy's deadpan observations and the running gag with the Underpass hermit. The emotional core - Chewy's gratitude despite his complaints - feels earned rather than sentimental, achieved through the pink-eye color reveal that maintains character consistency while showing vulnerability. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the Pratchett pastiche becomes slightly self-conscious, and some descriptions that edge toward being overly clever. However, these are minor quibbles in an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully balances humor with genuine heart. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
